item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed, it starts o get confusing and a bit convoluted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Even though the stuff described in this article was incredibly spoopy, the way this was written turned this into one of the most relaxing SCPs I ever read. To me, this SCP is the written equivalent of a Bob Ross episode.

+1 for cooking a nice bowl of [REDACTED] for my soul.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Someone already said it, but what is with this site and deer?

Marvelous. +1

I shudder to think what act 2 holds.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoting for well-done freaky format screw with a freaky deer, and somehow creeping me out with a recipe for stew. Also bonus points for the TDH reference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I saw the title "Act 1: The Lake South, The Deer North" on the main page I thought to myself 'hmm, is this a reference do TDH?'. Funny thing was, I had 'A Night on the Town' on when I stumbled upon it, and felt VERY satisfied. Great SCP btw, creepy as hell. Thinking about doing 'Act II: The Meaning of, and All Things Regarding Ms. Leeding'?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Epic writing! It's just like SCP-2521 where you can't speak or say anything about it. Btw my phone will no longer be with me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see what you did there! His hands matched his tongue.

edit: not more than 30 seconds after posting this I scrolled back up and actually read Popsioak's opening post, and realized I hadn't, in fact, spotted a small sly reference to an artist in a 3rd party's comment. I'll just pack my things up into my stick and bindle and be on my way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
May not be Volgun or Exploring, but we at Site-42 put this together. Hope people enjoy it.

SCP-5250: Act 1: The Lake South, The Deer North
https://youtu.be/ZbN-w6wK-2I

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm torn, because I think that the narrative parts of this, and the weaving in and out between the journal entries and the researcher's commentary were really well done. I just found almost all of the recipe/cooking references to feel kind of tacked on, and not of the same quality. Obviously due to the nature of the contest, that's what you had to work with, but I do think it's holding this piece back from being the best it can be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the formatting; I am doing an audio of this piece and the voice of the recipe was so fun to do.

In the second journal entry, I found what I think was an error and corrected it. Feel free to revert if I had the wrong idea.

“He's spent an awful lot of time back then,” was the original; I changed the “then” to “there.”

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If this is a recipe for anything, it's a recipe for a headache. Format screw just goes way too far IMO.
Oh wow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh yeah, I totally got that. But second person + whatnot can be weaved into conprocs without the recipe conceit, so it still doesn't quite jive with me. I do appreciate your efforts + the work you've clearly put in though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Recipe-wise: I do agree it was a bit difficult to tie the two together, though the in-universe reason does extend a bit further besides "it wants to be fed."

Specifically, the recipe + Neil's strange fascination with food + the fact the deer eats, then regurgitates, then pulls the fur traders underwater happens to come from the fact the lake itself doesn't quite understand the concept of typical feeding. Instead, it simply feeds off one's sense of self - not eating in the typical way, hence the whole "you-ness" that is consistently used throughout the article. By ceding that sense of "I," that lake cannot detect you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As the author has the first post + thus their author post is easily recognizable, the "spot" my comment is assigned to has literally no bearing on anything. It wouldn't matter if this comment was on page 1, or page 11, as it has, and will not have no important information that influences the skip itself.

This is a very odd hill to die on + I suggest you pick another one.

item.title: Wow. Just wow.
item.content:encoded:
This format screw is something else, and the fact that you managed to copy the con-procs style so damn smoothly makes it even better. Format screw aside, the dark and mysterious vibe of the article engulfed me throughout whole story and the anomaly itself was very interesting. I'm don't have an amazing attention-span, so I didn't figure out everything, however that doesn't make me like the scip itself.

I overall gotta say that this article in itself is what the corpsecon should be about. You have vote, and I hope + believe you will win this contest.

+1 Deer-ly enjoyed the article (I had to)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have no idea what is happening but I think my subconsciousness understood because all my body hair is standing on end.

Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dude, just comment when you want to comment, don't comment just to save yourself a spot. That's just… Not cool. I could go on a whole rant about it but it's probably best if I just leave it at that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean, ya ever see a deer irl? It’s a special occasion!

item.title: Ending
item.content:encoded:
Seems the ending was that the person identified as the lake, and was following the recipe the man contained —- the part calling for guards.

Completing the recipe was shooting the guards so the lake can eat it.

As such the whole "make it a recipe" containment procedure attempt is the lake tricking the people into providing it food.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looking forward to seeing this SCP get a reading by TheVolgun or maybe get explored on the Exploring series =p. Great use of playing around with the format, never thought I'd see it done in the form of a recipe. Very unique.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
what IS it with y'all and deer?

anyway +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This made me feel the same kind of creeped out that David Wong's books make me feel. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Placeholder to explain my thoughts on this when it's not early as hell.

Edit: WOW this was almost a week late! My sincere apologies. I really like the conceit of this, and I think the addenda was very well done - the testing logs + diary entries in particular. It was unsettling in the way that made me get up and close my closet door before sitting back down to keep reading. However - I don't think the recipe part ties in well. I know it's the hand you were dealt, but themes like that need to tie in thematically as well as plot-wise, and the tenuous justification of "it wants to be fed" doesn't quite do it for me - the lake entity just doesn't seem congruent with that manner of trickery imo. I could be reading wrong, but that's my current feeling, so it's a no-vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the worst pun I’ve seen

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really clever but I think it overstays its welcome. The first half of this was so good I cannot help but upvote the effort but this could probably do with a few pinches of minimalism in the latter half.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow! This was creepy and cool. I didn't get the ending, but it was still awesome. Nice work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Being a format stew can't save this article from being a more irritating version of SCP-2316 or SCP-2602.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know I've already voiced my opinion on this one directly, but I felt it worth commenting on the finished article too. I'm a huge fan of this one- you do great work with a difficult format-screw. If someone had told me a few days ago that I'd soon be reading a tense, engaging and narratively satisfying recipe for stew, would I have believed them? Probably not- and yet, here we are! A no-question +1 from me.
This is good format-screw surreal horror.

item.title: A NIGHT /// ON THE TOWN
item.content:encoded:
Like this piece? See it and many more saplings like it, at Popsioak's Garden, here!

i'm not who you think i am
and even if i thought you knew
i never would have told you so, and more alarming
I would have done the very same, would have stole more than your name
would have cursed and brought the world on your shoulders
i was in the wrong place, at the right time

Thanks to Laneous, DianaBerry, BlueJones, Meska, Mew-ltiverse, Doctor Fullham, TrustyOlValet2, Connor MacWarren and daviiid for polish and critique.

This is a bit of a wonky lil' thing for corpsecon. I think it came out pretty well, though.

Spoilers.

+ show block
– hide block

Basically, a big "lake" feeds off people's sense of self, using this wounded deer as a lure.
Thus, to contain it, it must be referred to as a recipe, and utilizing "You" - that way, it can't really tell you're talking about it.
It's a parallel story of both your POV, a Jr Researcher, and the people who first discovered the lake back when it was much, much smaller both falling prey to it.


image creds below :)
images
+ show block
– hide block

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Frozen_Lake_with_Footprints.jpg - CC BY SA 3.0
https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Whitetail-deer-buck-snow-falling_-_West_Virginia_-_ForestWander.jpg CC BY SA 3.0
